channel.title: KiddieDaily — Science
channel.description: Kid-friendly science and nature news, bias-rated.

item.title: London Climate Action Week Foiled by Extreme Heat
item.description: As record-breaking heat crushes Europe, organizers are moving events online to avoid exposing people to dangerously high temperatures.

item.title: French Startup Uses Special Polymers to Better Help Nerves Heal
item.description: The biodegradable material can help improve healing after surgery—or an avocado-related accident.

item.title: Even the Internet’s Favorite Pool Guy Doesn’t Know How to Fix the Reflecting Pool
item.description: Algae blooms, peeling paint, and a host of fixes from hydrogen peroxide to nanobubblers have made it hard to diagnose what’s wrong with the Reflecting Pool—let alone how to clean up the mess.

item.title: The ‘Parasite of Parasites’ Has Been Discovered in the Tropical Forests of Borneo
item.description: A newly identified species of fungus attacks the famous “zombie mushrooms” that control ants.

item.title: 3 People Have Gotten Cancer-Detecting Implants in Their Brains
item.description: Coherence Neuro has started testing a brain-computer interface that could one day use electrical stimulation to prevent tumors from growing.

item.title: A Source of Mysterious Repeating Radio Signals From Space Has Been Identified
item.description: Researchers say the discovery could be a “Rosetta stone” for cosmic signals.

item.title: The Most Promising Ebola Vaccine Has Been Sitting on the Shelf for 15 Years
item.description: Years after initial tests, researchers are now racing to see if a vaccine developed in 2011 can help fight the current Bundibugyo outbreak in Congo.

item.title: Canada Missed Chances to Inspect Titan Before Fatal Implosion
item.description: A new report shows that government agencies failed to communicate and includes recommendations for stronger oversight in a bid to avert future disasters.

item.title: West Antarctica Is Missing Way Too Much Ice
item.description: Temperatures have climbed up to 45 degrees Fahrenheit above normal, stopping ice from forming in the dead of Antarctic winter.

item.title: Why the Longest Match in Wimbledon History Lasted Three Days
item.description: The record will likely never be broken again.

item.title: Two Massive Earthquakes Struck Venezuela. Thousands Are Feared Dead
item.description: The country’s interim leader declared a state of emergency on Wednesday following shocks measuring 7.5 magnitude after two quakes hit in less than a minute.

item.title: Why the Reflecting Pool Is Full of Algae After Trump's Renovation
item.description: Warm weather has fueled a bloom that National Park Service workers are trying to kill using everything from hydrogen peroxide to nanobubbles ahead of July 4 celebrations.

item.title: An Alternative to Black Holes: Gravastars with Big Bangs Inside
item.description: Stellar mass black holes may not be black holes at all. Instead, they could be a type of extremely compact star called a gravastar, which mimics a black hole. This is according to theoretical phsyicis

item.title: World's Most Powerful Collider Shuts Down for a Smashing Upgrade
item.description: Europe's CERN physics research center bids 'Farewell' to the Large Hadron Collider, but it's actually more like 'See You Later, Accelerator!' The new, improved High-Luminosity LHC is due to make its d

item.title: ESA’s ExoMars Trace Gas Orbiter Has Yet to Detect Methane On Mars
item.description: After more than eight years of searching and with instruments designed to detect it, the European Space Agency’s (ESA) Mars Trace Gas Orbiter has yet to find methane in the red planet’s atmosphere.

item.title: The "Shadow Blaster" Galaxy's Role in High-energy Cosmic Neutrinos
item.description: On September 22, 2021, the IceCube Neutrino Detector in Antarctica caught a blast of neutrinos as it passed through the solar system. These neutrinos were remarkably high-energy and came from a galaxy

item.title: Mars May Have Vast Magma Systems Beneath Its Surface
item.description: Researchers from the University of Oxford have uncovered evidence that Mars once hosted widespread, Earth-like magmatic systems deep beneath its surface – despite the planet lacking the plate tectonic

item.title: Testing the Orbital Mechanics of Giant Mirrors
item.description: Giant mirrors in space have been a staple of science fiction for decades. But so far there’s been very little work looking at the actual physics behind the concept - possibly because we’re still so fa

item.title: The Rise of Space AI Might Explain the Fermi Paradox
item.description: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is continuing to have a disruptive impact on ever more parts of humanity. But what does it mean in the long run? A new paper, available in pre-print on arXiv from Austrian

item.title: The Black Holes That Burp Years After They Eat
item.description: When a star strays too close to a supermassive black hole, it is torn apart in a brief, brilliant flare, and astronomers long assumed that was the end of the story. It isn't. Using the Very Large Arra
